title: The reactor persists sync state in four PGlite tables: remotes, cursors, dead letters, and operation index.
content:
## Claim
Reactor sync state is durable across sessions because it lives in four dedicated PGlite tables.

## Tables (from source)
| Table | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sync_remotes` | Registered remotes: name, collection_id, channel_type, push/pull state |
| `sync_cursors` | Per-remote cursor tracking: ordinal position (inbox & outbox) |
| `sync_dead_letters` | Failed operations with error details |
| `operation_index_operations` | Global ordinal index across all operations |

## Why this matters
- Swarm remotes registered via `syncManager.add(...)` persist in `sync_remotes` and are recreated natively on startup — no re-registration code needed after reload.
- Cursors in `sync_cursors` provide exactly-once push semantics: the outbox cursor advances only after a successful remote write.
- `operation_index_operations` is the shared global ordinal space that lets multiple remotes coexist without stepping on each other.

## Source
Swarm Channel Architecture, "PGlite Tables" table.
description: sync_remotes, sync_cursors, sync_dead_letters, and operation_index_operations together let channels be recreated natively on startup.
